title: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Role of Campylobacter jejuni in Diarrheal Disease
items.content: Campylobacter jejuni is the principal pathogen responsible for diarrheal disease, accounting for 80-90% of all recognized illnesses due to Campylobacter and related genera. This highlights its significance in clinical settings where acute diarrhea is a presenting symptom.
keyPoints: Campylobacter jejuni accounts for 80–90% of all cases of recognized illness due to campylobacters and related genera.
Focus on C. jejuni as the major diarrheal pathogen when diagnosing acute diarrhea.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Recognizing Extraintestinal Infections by Campylobacter Species
items.content: While C. jejuni is primarily associated with diarrheal diseases, C. fetus is the major species causing extraintestinal illnesses. However, other campylobacters can also cause systemic or localized infections, particularly in compromised hosts.
keyPoints: C. fetus is the primary pathogen for extraintestinal infections among Campylobacter species.
Other diarrheal agents may cause systemic or localized infection, especially in immunocompromised individuals.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission and Incidence of Campylobacter Infections in Developed Countries
items.content: Campylobacters are primarily transmitted to humans through raw or undercooked poultry, accounting for 30–70% of cases. Other transmission routes include unpasteurized milk, untreated water, contact with infected pets, contaminated seafood, travel to developing countries, oral-anal sexual contact, and cross-contamination.
keyPoints: Ingestion of contaminated poultry is the most common mode of acquisition (30–70% of cases).
Incidence of campylobacter diarrheal disease in the U.S. is ~20 cases per 100,000 persons annually.
Peak incidence occurs during summer and early autumn.
Highest attack rates for C. jejuni are among young children and young adults.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Risk Factors and Epidemiology of Campylobacter Infections in Developing Countries
items.content: In developing countries with poor sanitation, C. jejuni infections are hyperendemic, especially among children under 2 years old. These infections can lead to stunting and a specific microbiome signature.
keyPoints: C. jejuni infections are hyperendemic in many developing countries.
Highest rates of infection occur in children <2 years old.
Asymptomatic C. jejuni infections are associated with short stature (stunting) and a particular microbiome signature.
Rates of clinically apparent infection decrease with age due to the development of immunity.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Timing and Sites of Tissue Injury in Campylobacter jejuni Infections
items.content: Campylobacter jejuni infections typically present symptoms within 2–4 days after exposure, though this can range from 1 to 7 days. The primary sites affected are the jejunum, ileum, and colon, with biopsy findings showing acute inflammatory reactions involving neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, and epithelial damage.
keyPoints: Symptomatic infections occur within 2–4 days (range, 1–7 days) of exposure.
Tissue injury primarily affects the jejunum, ileum, and colon.

Biopsy findings include acute inflammatory reactions with neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils in the lamina propria, and epithelial damage such as loss of mucus, glandular degeneration, and crypt abscesses.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Pathogenesis and Immune Response in Campylobacter jejuni Infections
items.content: The pathogenesis of C. jejuni infections involves motility and adherence to host tissues, with tissue invasion being clinically significant. The role of antibodies is suggested by the severity and recurrence of infections in immunoglobulin-deficient patients. However, classic enterotoxins and cytotoxins are not major contributors to disease.
keyPoints: Motility and adherence to host tissues favor disease development.
Tissue invasion is significant, with organisms visualized within the epithelium and evidence of C. jejuni bacteremia.
Antibodies likely play a role in protective immunity, as suggested by infection severity in immunoglobulin-deficient patients.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Prodromal Symptoms of Campylobacter Infection
items.content: Campylobacter infections often begin with a prodrome characterized by fever, headache, myalgia, and/or malaise occurring 12–48 hours before the onset of diarrheal symptoms. This early presentation can mimic other febrile illnesses such as typhoid fever.
keyPoints: Prodromal phase includes fever, headache, myalgia, and malaise.
Occurs 12–48 hours prior to diarrhea onset.
Fever may be the only initial manifestation, mimicking early stages of typhoid fever.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Diarrheal Phase and Complications in Campylobacter Infection
items.content: The intestinal phase of Campylobacter infection typically presents with diarrhea, abdominal pain, and fever. Diarrhea can range from loose stools to bloody stools (~10% in adults), with most patients experiencing ≥10 bowel movements on the worst day. Abdominal pain is often cramping and may localize, leading to pseudoappendicitis. Complications such as myocarditis and pericarditis are more common in young men.
keyPoints: Diarrhea varies from loose to bloody stools (~10% of cases in adults).
Most patients have ≥10 bowel movements on the worst day.
Abdominal pain is usually cramping, may localize causing pseudoappendicitis.
Complications include myocarditis and pericarditis, especially in young men.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Understanding the Patterns and Management of Campylobacter Bacteremia
items.content: Campylobacter bacteremia, primarily caused by C. jejuni and C. fetus, presents in three patterns: transient bacteremia with benign course, sustained bacteremia or focal infection responding well to antimicrobials, and sustained bacteremia in compromised hosts requiring immediate and possibly prolonged therapy.
keyPoints: 90% of bacteremic infections are caused by C. jejuni and C. fetus.
Mortality rate is ~12%.
Bacteremia is uncommon except in compromised hosts (immunocompromised, diabetic, extremes of age).
Three patterns: transient bacteremia with benign course; sustained bacteremia or focal infection responding to antimicrobials; sustained bacteremia in compromised hosts requiring immediate therapy.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Postinfectious Sequelae of Campylobacter Infections: Reactive Arthritis and Guillain-Barré Syndrome
items.content: Reactive arthritis occurs in up to 2.5% of Campylobacter cases, with nonspecific rheumatologic symptoms more common (~10%). Guillain-Barré syndrome follows symptomatic or asymptomatic infections at a rate of 1 in 1000–2000 cases, but for certain C. jejuni serotypes (O19), it occurs in 1 in 100–200 cases.
keyPoints: Reactive arthritis reported in up to 2.5% of Campylobacter cases; nonspecific rheumatologic symptoms ~10%.
Guillain-Barré syndrome follows infections at a rate of 1 in 1000–2000 cases, or 1 in 100–200 for serotype O19.
Campylobacter may trigger 20-40% of all Guillain-Barré syndrome cases due to molecular mimicry.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Diagnosis of Campylobacter Enteritis: Leukocytes and Culture Techniques
items.content: In diagnosing Campylobacter enteritis, peripheral leukocyte counts can indicate the severity of inflammation. Stool samples from patients typically contain leukocytes or erythrocytes. Direct microscopic examination using Gram's staining or phase-contrast/dark-field microscopy helps identify organisms by their characteristic morphology and motility. Cultures on specific media are essential for confirming diagnosis.
keyPoints: Peripheral leukocyte counts reflect inflammation severity in Campylobacter enteritis.
Stools from nearly all infected patients contain leukocytes or erythrocytes.
Direct microscopic examination can identify organisms by vibrioid morphology and 'darting' motility.
Confirmation requires isolation of an organism from stool, blood, or another site; species identification possible via MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry.
Campylobacter-specific media are necessary for culturing stools with inflammatory or bloody diarrhea.
Culture is the gold standard but PCR techniques may be more sensitive.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Use of PCR and CIDTs in Campylobacter Diagnosis: Sensitivity vs. Specificity
items.content: While culture remains the diagnostic gold standard for Campylobacter, species-specific real-time PCR techniques offer higher sensitivity. Culture-independent diagnostic tests (CIDTs) like PCR or antigen detection can detect nonviable bacteria, leading to potential false positives. However, they are increasingly used in clinical settings due to their sensitivity.
keyPoints: PCR and CIDTs may be more sensitive than culture for detecting Campylobacter.
Culture-independent tests might detect nonviable bacteria, causing false positives.
CIDT positivity does not always imply active or recent infection.
Follow-up testing post-clinical resolution of acute infection is rarely needed.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis: Campylobacter Enteritis vs. Other Conditions
items.content: Campylobacter enteritis can mimic inflammatory bowel diseases like ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, but it is more common among young adults. Biopsy may not distinguish between these conditions; thus, ruling out Campylobacter infection is crucial before diagnosing inflammatory bowel disease.
keyPoints: Campylobacter enteritis symptoms are similar to those caused by Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia, and E. coli.
Fever with fecal leukocytes or erythrocytes indicates inflammatory diarrhea.
Culture, CIDTs, or stained fecal smears confirm Campylobacter infection.
Campylobacter enteritis is more common than ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease among young adults.
Diagnosis of inflammatory bowel disease should await exclusion of Campylobacter, especially with risk factors like foreign travel.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS

items.title: Antimicrobial Therapy Indications for Campylobacter Enteritis
items.content: Specific antimicrobial therapy is not always necessary for Campylobacter enteritis. It should be considered when there are high fever, bloody or severe diarrhea, persistence beyond one week, or worsening symptoms.
keyPoints: Not all patients benefit from specific antimicrobial treatment.
Indications include high fever, bloody diarrhea, severe diarrhea, persistence >1 week, and symptom worsening.
Azithromycin (500 mg daily for 3 days) is the preferred regimen; a single dose of 1000 mg can also be used.
~27% of U.S. Campylobacter isolates were resistant to ciprofloxacin in 2014.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS

items.title: Prognosis and Treatment of Systemic Campylobacter Infections
items.content: Systemic infections, particularly with C. fetus, have a higher mortality rate than other species. Rapid initiation of appropriate therapy is crucial for prognosis. Immunocompromised patients may require prolonged or lifelong therapy.
keyPoints: Nearly all patients recover from Campylobacter enteritis spontaneously or after treatment.
Volume depletion contributes to rare fatalities.
Systemic infections with C. fetus are more fatal, especially in compromised hosts.
Rapid appropriate therapy improves prognosis; otherwise healthy individuals usually survive without sequelae.
Immunocompromised patients may need prolonged (up to 4 weeks) or lifelong therapy for systemic infections.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which genus does Campylobacter belong to, based on its morphology?
options: Vibrio-like organisms due to their curved rod shape
items.explanation: Campylobacter is named for its vibrio-like morphology, meaning 'curved rod'.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which species of Campylobacter is the principal diarrheal pathogen?
items.explanation: Campylobacter jejuni accounts for 80–90% of all recognized cases of campylobacter-related diarrheal illness.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which species is primarily associated with extraintestinal infections?
items.explanation: The major species causing extraintestinal illnesses is C. fetus.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is the most common mode of acquisition for Campylobacter infections in developed countries?
options: Ingestion of contaminated poultry that has not been sufficiently cooked
Ingestion of raw (unpasteurized) milk or untreated water
items.explanation: In developed countries, ingestion of undercooked poultry is the most common mode of Campylobacter transmission.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which group has the highest attack rates for C. jejuni infections?
items.explanation: C. jejuni infections have the highest attack rates among young children and young adults.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a risk factor for systemic Campylobacter infections?
items.explanation: Systemic infections are more common among compromised hosts, not healthy nonpregnant persons.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which sites are primarily involved in tissue injury during a Campylobacter jejuni infection?
items.explanation: The passage states that the sites of tissue injury include the jejunum, ileum, and colon.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of C. fetus that contributes to its ability to cause bacteremia?
options: High motility and adherence to host tissues
items.explanation: The passage mentions that C. fetus possesses a proteinaceous capsule-like structure (an S-layer) that renders the organisms resistant to complement-mediated killing and opsonization.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is suggested about the role of antibodies in protective immunity against Campylobacter jejuni?
options: Antibodies are crucial for long-term immunity
Antibodies provide complete protection without exposure
Antibodies may be important but protection can be short-lived
items.explanation: The passage suggests that antibodies are important in protective immunity, as indicated by the severity and recurrence of infections among immunoglobulin-deficient patients, yet protection may be short-lived or incomplete without continuous exposure.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which symptom is most commonly associated with the intestinal phase of Campylobacter jejuni infection?
items.explanation: The most common signs and symptoms of the intestinal phase are diarrhea, abdominal pain, and fever.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is a potential complication of C. fetus infection in compromised hosts?
items.explanation: C. fetus infections have a tropism for vascular sites: endocarditis, mycotic aneurysm, and septic thrombophlebitis may all occur.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which symptom might mimic the early stages of typhoid fever in C. jejuni infection?
items.explanation: Fever may be the only initial manifestation of C. jejuni infection, a situation mimicking the early stages of typhoid fever.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which organism is most commonly responsible for bacteremic infections caused by Campylobacter?
items.explanation: About 90% of bacteremic infections are caused by C. jejuni and C. fetus, in roughly equal proportions.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is the estimated incidence of Guillain-Barré syndrome following symptomatic or asymptomatic Campylobacter infections?
items.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome or its Miller Fisher variant follow either symptomatic or asymptomatic Campylobacter infections uncommonly—i.e., in 1 of every 1000–2000 cases.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which postinfectious sequelae is most commonly associated with Campylobacter infections?
items.explanation: The two most common postinfectious sequelae are reactive arthritis and Guillain-Barré syndrome, with reactive arthritis reported in up to 2.5% of cases.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which microscopy technique is used to identify the characteristic 'darting' motility of Campylobacter organisms?
items.explanation: Phase-contrast or dark-field microscopy is used to identify the characteristic 'darting' motility of Campylobacter organisms.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is considered the diagnostic gold standard for confirming Campylobacter infection?
items.explanation: Culture remains the diagnostic gold standard for confirming Campylobacter infection.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which method is more sensitive than culture for detecting specific species of Campylobacter?
items.explanation: Species-specific real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR) techniques appear more sensitive than culture for detecting specific species of Campylobacter.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition should be ruled out before diagnosing inflammatory bowel disease in patients with symptoms of diarrhea, especially those with a history of foreign travel or significant animal contact?
items.explanation: Campylobacter enteritis may mimic inflammatory bowel diseases like ulcerative colitis and should be ruled out first.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS

items.question: Which antibiotic regimen is considered the treatment of choice for Campylobacter enteritis?
options: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 3 days
Azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days
items.explanation: A 3-day course of azithromycin is the regimen of choice due to lower resistance rates.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS

items.question: In which setting should infection due to C. fetus specifically be suspected?
items.explanation: C. fetus should be suspected in the setting of septic abortion.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended for patients with Campylobacter enteritis due to potential severe complications?
items.explanation: The use of antimotility agents is not recommended as they may prolong symptoms and lead to toxic megacolon.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS

items.explanation: Campylobacters are non-spore-forming, curved, gram-negative rods.

items.subtopic: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Helicobacter cinaedi is an intestinal organism.
items.explanation: H. cinaedi, along with H. fennelliae, are intestinal organisms causing diarrheal disease.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Campylobacter infections peak during winter and late autumn.
items.explanation: Infections peak during summer and early autumn, not in winter.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: C. jejuni infections are associated with short stature (stunting) even when asymptomatic.
items.explanation: Asymptomatic C. jejuni infections in low- to middle-income countries are linked to stunting.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Classic enterotoxins and cytotoxins play substantial roles in tissue injury during Campylobacter jejuni infection.
items.explanation: The passage states that classic enterotoxins and cytotoxins appear not to play substantial roles in tissue injury or disease production.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: C. fetus infections are characterized by the organism's ability to switch S-layer proteins, contributing to antigenic variability.
items.explanation: The passage describes that C. fetus can switch the S-layer proteins expressed, resulting in antigenic variability.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Campylobacter enteritis is generally self-limited and symptoms persist for more than one week in over 50% of patients seeking medical attention.
items.explanation: Symptoms persist for >1 week in 10–20% of patients seeking medical attention.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: C. fetus can cause a prolonged relapsing systemic illness with bacteremia that has no obvious primary source, especially common among compromised hosts.
items.explanation: This manifestation is especially common among compromised hosts.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Reactive arthritis associated with Campylobacter infections is linked with the HLA-B27 phenotype.
items.explanation: There is no association between reactive arthritis and the HLA-B27 phenotype.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Guillain-Barré syndrome following Campylobacter infections can be triggered by molecular mimicry involving sialylated lipopolysaccharides.
items.explanation: The presence of sialylated lipopolysaccharides on C. jejuni strains prompts a form of molecular mimicry that promotes autoimmune recognition.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Campylobacter bacteremia is easily detected using standard blood culture media.
items.explanation: Campylobacter species have low levels of metabolic activity in standard blood culture media, making detection difficult.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: CIDT positivity for Campylobacter always implies active or recent infection.
items.explanation: CIDT positivity is more questionable and may detect nonviable bacteria, leading to falsely positive results.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Resistance to fluoroquinolones among U.S. human isolates of Campylobacter was approximately 27% in 2014.
items.explanation: The passage states that ~27% of U.S. human isolates were resistant to ciprofloxacin in 2014.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS

items.statement: Most patients with systemic infection due to C. fetus recover without any treatment.
items.explanation: Systemic infections, especially those caused by C. fetus, require appropriate antibiotics and prolonged therapy.
items.reference: Infections Due to Campylobacter and Related Organisms · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Campylobacter Infection | PROGNOSIS
